q0001: The 'E' in MRS GREN stands for [excretion]: an organism's ability to get rid of waste.
q0002: The 'G' in MRS GREN stands for [growth]: an organism's ability to grow in mass and height.
q0003: The 'M' in MRS GREN stands for [movement]: an organism's ability to move.
q0004: The 'N' in MRS GREN stands for [nutrition]: an organism's ability to get nutrients from food.
q0009: A group of tissues working together for a specific function is called an [organ].
q0010: As well as holding the plant in place, roots also [absorb water and minerals from the soil] from the soil.
q0011: The main organ of nutrition in a plant is the [leaf].
q0012: A group of similar cells working together for a specific function is called a [tissue].
q0013: Two tissues found in the heart are [muscle and fat (accept nerve as well)].
q0014: The role of xylem tissue is to [transport water] up through the plant.
q0015: A cell that has its DNA contained within a nucleus is called a [eukaryotic cell].
q0016: An adaptation of a palisade cell is having lots of [chloroplasts] to absorb light.
q0017: The 5 sub-cellular organelles in an animal cell: [nucleus, cell membrane, ribosomes, cytoplasm, mitochondria].
q0018: The 8 sub-cellular organelles in a plant cell: [nucleus, cell membrane, ribosomes, cytoplasm, mitochondria, cell wall, chloroplast and vacuole].
q0043: The six organ systems in the body: [circulatory, nervous, muscular, respiratory, digestive, skeletal].
q0044: From smallest to largest: cell < [tissue] < [organ] < [organ system] < organism.
q0045: The role of the circulatory system is to [transport substances around our body] substances around the body.
q0046: The joining of the nuclei of a male and female sex cell is called [fertilisation].
q0047: Sex cells (like sperm and eggs) are called [gamete].
q0048: External fertilisation occurs [occurs] the body of the parents.
q0049: Internal fertilisation occurs [internal] the body of the female.
q0051: The 3 key parts of the male reproductive system: the [testis, sperm duct, penis].
q0052: The 5 key parts of the female reproductive system: [ovary, fallopian tubes, cervix, uterus, vagina].
q0053: The time when a woman's ovaries stop releasing egg cells is called [menopause].
q0054: Sperm cells are made in the [testes].
q0055: Two substances that pass from the baby to the mother's blood via the placenta: [oxygen and nutrients (or named nutrient)].
q0056: Two substances that pass from the mother's blood to the baby via the placenta: [carbon dioxide and waste products (or urea)].
q0057: The role of amniotic fluid is to [protect the baby] the developing baby.
q0059: The time taken for a baby to develop from fertilisation to birth is called the [gestation period].
q0060: The organ that provides the foetus with oxygen and nutrients is called the [placenta].
q0061: The tube that connects the foetus to the placenta is called the [umbilical cord].
q0062: The monthly shedding of the uterus lining is called [menstruation].
q0063: The release of an egg from the ovary is called [ovulation].
q0064: The hormone that causes egg maturation in the ovaries is [fSH (follicle stimulating hormone)] (follicle stimulating hormone).
q0065: The hormone that maintains the uterus lining is [progesterone].
q0074: When we breathe in, the diaphragm [contracts and flattens].
q0075: When we breathe out, the diaphragm [relaxes] and returns to a [dome] shape.
q0076: The three types of blood vessel are [arteries, veins and capillaries].
q0077: The function of the ribcage is to [protect the heart and lungs] the heart and lungs.
q0080: The purpose of white blood cells is to [protect] the body against [infection].
q0081: Blood cells are made in the [bone marrow].
q0082: The 4 main functions of the skeleton: [support], [protect] vital organs, enable [movement] and making [blood cells].
q0083: The smooth substance found at the end of bones is called [cartilage].
q0084: A hip joint is an example of a [ball and socket] joint.
q0085: The mineral that makes bones strong is [calcium].
q0088: An example of antagonistic muscles in the legs: the [quadriceps and hamstrings].
q0090: Muscles are attached to bone by strong [tendons].
q0091: Muscles work by getting shorter — a process called [contraction].
q0095: An example of a [depressant] drug (one that slows the nervous system) is alcohol.
q0096: An example of a [hallucinogen] (a drug that causes hallucinations) is MDMA or marijuana.
q0097: An example of a [painkiller] drug is ibuprofen or morphine.
q0098: An example of a [stimulant] drug (one that speeds the nervous system) is caffeine or tobacco.
q0099: The environment that an organism lives in is called its [habitat].
q0100: Two examples of [continuous] variation in humans are height and weight.
q0101: Two examples of [discontinuous] variation in humans are blood type and eye colour.
q0102: All the living and non-living things in a given area make up an [ecosystem].
q0103: Organisms that can interbreed to produce fertile offspring belong to the same [species].
q0104: Inherited variation refers to [characteristics] passed on from your [parents], such as eye colour.
q0105: Three adaptations that help a tiger survive: [camouflage], sharp [teeth] and [claws], and [forward]-facing eyes.
q0106: Two seasonal adaptations in animals include [hibernation, shedding winter coat, migration, etc.].
q0108: Deciduous trees lose their leaves in winter to reduce [water loss and nutrient expenditure] and save energy.
q0109: An organism that only eats other animals is called a [carnivore].
q0110: When organisms fight for a share of limited resources, this is called [competition].
q0111: An organism that feeds on other organisms is called a [consumer].
q0112: Organisms that break down dead material and return nutrients to the soil are called [decomposer].
q0126: The process where toxins build up to higher concentrations further along a food chain is called [bioaccumulation].
q0127: In a pyramid of biomass, the producer (e.g. [rose]) always forms the widest bar at the base.
q0128: In a pyramid of numbers for grass → grasshopper → frog → snake, the bar for [grass] is widest at the base.
q0129: In chemistry, a substance made of only one element or compound is called [pure].
q0130: In everyday language, a pure substance is one that has had [nothing] added to it.
q0131: The two pieces of equipment needed for filtration: a [for].
q0132: To separate an insoluble solid from a liquid we use [filtration].
q0135: Three ways to speed up dissolving: [stir and heat] and grinding into smaller pieces.
q0145: To separate a dissolved solid from a solution we use [evaporation].
q0146: The boiling point of water is [100] °C.
q0147: Unlike boiling, evaporation can occur at [and].
q0148: Two errors in chromatography: drawing the start line with a [pen], and solvent level being [above] the sample line.
q0149: To separate a mixture of coloured dyes we use [chromatography].
q0150: The start line on a chromatogram is drawn in [pencil] so it doesn't [dissolve] in the solvent.
q0151: The 5 pieces of equipment for evaporation: [equipment].
q0152: Distillation has two stages: first [heating] to evaporate the liquid, then [cooling] to condense the vapour.
q0153: To separate a solvent from a solution we use [distillation].
q0154: The hazard symbol for flammable substances shows a [for].
q0155: A [corrosive] substance reacts with metals and organic materials, wearing them away.
q0156: The exclamation mark hazard symbol represents something [irritant/harmful].
q0157: Two examples of indicators are [litmus] paper and [universal indicator].
q0158: Universal indicator turns [red] in a strong acid.
q0159: Universal indicator turns [orange] in a weak acid.
q0160: A substance that changes colour in acid or alkali is called an [indicator].
q0161: Universal indicator turns [purple] in an alkali.
q0162: Universal indicator turns [green] in a neutral substance.
q0163: A neutral substance has a pH of [pH 7].
q0164: A strong acid has a pH of between [pH 1-3].
q0168: The reaction between an acid and an alkali to form a salt and water is called [neutralisation].
q0169: The general equation for neutralisation is: [acid] + [alkali] → [salt] + [water].
q0170: The chemical formula for hydrochloric acid is [hCl].
q0171: The chemical formula for sodium hydroxide is [naOH].
q0174: When magnesium hydroxide reacts with hydrochloric acid, the products are [magnesium chloride and water].
q0175: Bleach is [alkaline] — it has a pH above 7.
q0176: Toothpaste is [alkaline] — it has a pH above 7.
q0177: The property measured in metres cubed (m³) is [volume].
q0178: Of the three states of matter, only [gases only.] can be compressed.
q0179: The two states of matter that can flow are [liquids and gases].
q0180: The temperature at which a liquid turns into a gas is called the [boiling point].
q0181: Particles in a gas move [quick and random].
q0182: Particles in a liquid can [slide over each other] over each other.
q0183: Particles in a solid only [vibrate] on the spot.
q0203: The random movement of microscopic particles in a fluid is called [Brownian motion].
q0205: Brownian motion occurs in [liquids and gases].
q0206: The movement of particles from low to high concentration using energy is called [active transport].
q0207: The movement of particles from high to low concentration is called [diffusion].
q0208: The movement of water through a partially permeable membrane from a dilute to concentrated solution is called [osmosis].
q0209: Diffusion does not occur in a [solid].
q0210: When a gas is heated, its pressure [increases].
q0211: Pumping air into a tyre increases pressure because there are more [particles] causing more [collisions] with the walls.
q0212: Sucking on a straw lowers [pressure] inside it, so higher pressure outside [pushes] the liquid up.
q0213: The tiny building blocks that make up all matter are called [atom].
q0214: A group of atoms chemically bonded together is called a [molecule].
q0215: Substances made of only one type of atom are called [elements].
q0216: Substances made of two or more types of atoms chemically bonded together are called [compounds].
q0223: The chemical symbol for aluminium is [al].
q0224: The chemical symbol for argon is [ar].
q0225: The chemical symbol for beryllium is [be].
q0226: The chemical symbol for boron is [b].
q0243: Three magnetic elements are [iron, nickel and cobalt.].
q0244: Four properties of most metals: [high] melting point, good [conductor] of heat and electricity, [ductile] and [malleable].
q0245: Four properties of most non-metals: [low] melting point, [brittle] when solid, not [shiny], poor [conductor].
q0246: When chlorine gas reacts with copper, [copper chloride] is formed.
q0247: Substances made of a metal and non-metal chemically bonded together usually end in [-ide]-ide.
q0248: A reaction that releases heat energy to the surroundings is called [exothermic].
q0249: Three signs of a chemical reaction: [colour] change, change in [temperature] and [fizzing].
q0250: Substances made of a metal, a non-metal and oxygen chemically bonded together usually end in [-ate]-ate.
q0251: A reaction in which [heat] is used to break down one substance into products is called [thermal decomposition].
q0252: The unit used to measure the amount of energy available in food is called a [calorie].
q0253: The standard unit for energy is the [joule (J)].
q0254: The energy stored in food is released by [combustion or respiration].
q0255: Kilojoules are used on food labels because the energy values in joules would be very [labels].
q0256: The process of burning fuel to release heat and light energy is called [combustion].
q0257: An example of a store of chemical energy is a [battery, Food].
q0258: [respiration] is the chemical reaction that uses glucose and oxygen to release energy in cells.
q0259: Approximately [three quarters] of UK electricity is generated from fossil fuels.
q0260: A resource that is in limited supply and cannot be replaced is called a [finite" resource] resource.
q0261: An energy resource that cannot be replaced once used up is called [non-renewable].
q0262: An energy source that can be naturally replaced and will not run out is called [renewable' energy source].
q0272: Six renewable energy resources: [solar, wind, waves, hydroelectric, geothermal, biomass].
q0273: All the energy found in our food originally comes from the [Sun].
q0274: Biofuels can be considered renewable because we can [plant more of them] more of the source material.
q0275: An advantage of nuclear fuels: they do not produce [sulphur dioxide or carbon dioxide].
q0276: Disadvantages of nuclear fuels: [non-renewable], [radioactive] waste and risk of accidents.
q0277: Two disadvantages of fossil fuels: they release [greenhouse gases] and are [non-renewable].
q0278: The flow of electric charge around a circuit is called [current].
q0279: The unit of electric current is the [ampere (Amp)] (amp).
q0280: Electric current is measured using an [ammeter].
q0281: Examples of electrical conductors include [metal elements, graphite, mixtures of metal, salt solution, liquid calcium chloride].
q0282: Examples of electrical insulators include [plastic], [wood] and most [non-metals].
q0283: To test conductivity, set up a [series] circuit with a lamp — if the lamp lights, the material is a [conductor].
q0284: A material that allows electric charge to move easily through it is called an electrical [conductor].
q0286: A circuit where components are on separate branches is called a [parallel" circuit] circuit.
q0287: A circuit where all components are connected in a single loop is called a [series" circuit] circuit.
q0288: In a parallel circuit, the total current equals the [state] of the currents in each branch.
q0289: In a series circuit, the current is the [same at every point in the circuit and in every component] at every point in the circuit.
q0292: Conductors have [low] resistance; insulators have [high] resistance.
q0293: The energy transferred per unit charge flowing around a circuit is called [potential difference" (or "voltage")] (or voltage).
q0294: The property that opposes and slows down the flow of electric current is called [resistance].
q0295: The unit of potential difference is the [volt (V)].
q0298: In a UK plug: [blue] = neutral, [brown] = live, [green]/[yellow] stripes = earth.
q0299: Three safety features of a UK plug: [insulation] on the prongs, a [cable grip] and a [fuse].
q0300: A mains plug needs a [fuse] to melt and break the circuit if too much current flows.
q0301: A force that only acts when two objects are physically [touching] is called a [contact force].
q0302: A push or pull that acts on an object due to an interaction is called a [force].
q0303: A force that can act between objects without them needing to touch is called a [non-contact force].
q0304: The push force that acts perpendicularly between two solid objects in contact is called the [normal contact force].
q0309: A force that squashes or pushes objects together is called [compression].
q0310: A force that stretches or pulls an object apart is called [tension].
q0311: The point at which a material permanently deforms and stops returning to its original shape is the [elastic limit].
q0312: Forces are measured using a [newton meter].
q0316: The friction force acting on an object as it moves through the air is called [air resistance].
q0317: The friction force acting on an object moving through a liquid or gas is called [drag].
q0318: The force that opposes motion between two surfaces in contact is called [friction].
q0319: Two situations where reducing friction is useful: in [sport] and in [vehicles].
q0321: Increasing the surface area of shoes [decreases] the pressure exerted on the ground.
q0322: The equation for pressure is: P = F ÷ [pressure = Force/Area].
q0323: The unit of pressure is the [pascal, Pa (accept N/m^2 or N/cm^2)] (Pa).
q0324: The single force that has the same effect as all individual forces combined is called the [resultant force].
q0325: When forces are unbalanced, an object may [accelerate], [decelerate], change [speed] or change [direction].
q0326: When forces are balanced, an object either stays at [rest] or moves at [constant speed].
q0327: If all forces on an object are balanced, the resultant force is [zero].
q0328: The maximum displacement of a wave from its resting position is called its [amplitude].
q0329: The number of complete waves produced per second, measured in Hertz, is called [frequency].
q0330: The [pitch] of a sound (how high or low it sounds) is determined by its [frequency].
q0331: The [volume] of a sound (how loud or quiet it is) is determined by its [amplitude].
q0333: The mid-point of a wave's vibration, where particles return to rest, is called the [resting point"/"rarefaction].
q0334: A region of space that contains no matter or particles at all is called a [vacuum].
q0335: The speed of sound in air is [330] m/s.
q0336: The wave equation is: wave speed = [frequency] × [wavelength].
q0337: The range of sound frequencies that an organism can detect is called its [auditory range].
q0338: Sound waves with a frequency above the human hearing range are called [ultrasound].
q0339: The six parts of the ear: [ear drum], [bones], [cochlea], [auditory nerve], [semi-circular canals] and [pinna].
q0340: The three tiny bones in the middle ear are the [hammer, anvil, stirrup].
q0348: Echolocation works by sending out a sound pulse that [reflects] off objects — the return time reveals the [distance].
q0349: Being able to hear [ultrasound] benefits moths (that cannot echolocate) because they can detect predators like [bats].
q0350: When sound reaches the boundary between two media, it can be [reaches].
q0351: In a longitudinal wave, the direction of vibration is [parallel] to the direction the wave travels.
q0352: In a transverse wave, the direction of vibration is [perpendicular/at] (at right angles) to the direction the wave travels.
q0353: When two waves overlap, we say they are in [superposition].
q0354: Biuret solution is used to test for [proteins].
q0355: The 7 food groups: [carbohydrates, proteins, fats, fibre, vitamins, minerals, water].
q0357: Two examples of foods rich in carbohydrates are [rice and pasta].
q0358: Two examples of foods rich in fibre are [brown bread and cereal].
q0359: Three examples of foods rich in fat are [butter, cheese and chocolate].
q0360: Foods rich in minerals and vitamins include [fruit and vegetables].
q0362: An eating disorder where a person severely restricts food intake to lose weight is called [anorexia].
q0363: Eating the right variety and amounts of each food group is called a [balanced diet].
q0364: Three reasons for needing more calories: being very [active], [growing], or being [pregnant].
q0365: Three diseases associated with an unbalanced diet: [obesity], [anorexia] and type 2 [diabetes].
q0366: The 7 main organs of the digestive system: [mouth, oesophagus, stomach, pancreas, liver, small intestine, large intestine].
q0367: [Water] is absorbed from the large intestine.
q0368: [Nutrients] are absorbed from the small intestine.
q0369: When proteins are digested, [amino acids] are produced.
q0382: The spreading of particles from high to low concentration until evenly spread is called [diffusion].
q0383: The small intestine has a large surface area due to tiny finger-like projections called [villi].
q0384: Villi aid absorption because they have [thin] cell walls, a network of [capillaries] and a large [surface area].
q0385: Unlike chemical changes, physical changes do not involve a chemical [reaction] and can often be [reversed].
q0386: Evaporation is a [physical change] change — no new substances are created.
q0387: Dalton's atomic model stated all matter is made of indestructible [atoms], identical within each [element].
q0388: Carbon hydride with a 1:4 carbon-to-hydrogen ratio has the formula [cH4].
q0389: In carbon dioxide (CO₂), the ratio of carbon to oxygen atoms is [1:2].
q0390: When 4g of zinc reacts with 1g of oxygen, conservation of mass means [5] g of zinc oxide is produced.
q0391: The vertical columns in the periodic table are called [group].
q0392: Elements in the periodic table are arranged in order of increasing [are].
q0393: The number of electrons in a neutral atom equals its [atomic].
q0402: The horizontal rows in the periodic table are called [period].
q0403: Four properties of metals: [high] melting point, good [conductors], [ductile] and [malleable].
q0404: Four properties of non-metals: [low] melting point, [brittle] when solid, not [shiny], poor [conductors].
q0405: The 5 halogens, lowest to highest reactivity: [astatine, Iodine, Bromine, Chlorine, Fluorine].
q0413: The 6 alkali metals, lowest to highest reactivity: [lithium, sodium, potassium, rubidium, caesium, francium].
q0414: As you move down group 1, reactivity [increases].
q0415: When alkali metals react with water, [metals] is formed (plus hydrogen gas).
q0416: When freshly cut, alkali metals appear [shiny].
